attack_surface.exposure: HTTP client handles responses from arbitrary external servers — downloads, API responses, SSE streams
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Every HTTP response body, header, and stream chunk is untrusted external data
mitigations: 100MB size limit on in-memory response reads (ReadLimitedStringAsync/ReadLimitedBytesAsync)
SSE buffer overflow disconnect after 3 consecutive overflows
Minimum throughput check (1KB/sec over 30s) on all stream reads
URL scheme validation — only http:// and https:// allowed
gaps: No SSRF protection against internal IP ranges (127.0.0.1, 169.254.x.x, 10.x.x.x)
Throughput threshold (1KB/sec) is generous — allows 86MB/day per connection
Deserialization of application/plang responses occurs before signature verification

attack_surface.exposure: Fluid templates render with full access to app object graph via UnsafeMemberAccessStrategy
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Template content and variable values — if any come from external sources, attacker controls template expressions
gaps: UnsafeMemberAccessStrategy allows access to ALL public properties (accepted risk)
callGoal tag can execute arbitrary goals from template expressions (accepted risk)

attack_surface.exposure: Data navigators parse JSON strings from HTTP responses, file reads, and variable resolution
attack_surface.trust_boundary: JSON content from external APIs stored in variables, then navigated via dot notation
mitigations: MaxDepth = 64 explicit depth guard on UnwrapElement

attack_surface.exposure: Ed25519 signature verification for Data integrity and identity
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Signed data arrives over transport — signature, nonce, headers are attacker-controlled until verified
mitigations: Nonce replay detection via cache with TTL matching signature timeout
gaps: Nonce cache is in-memory — bounded by timeout check, but window exists during restart

attack_surface.exposure: Variable resolution processes values that may originate from external data (HTTP responses stored in variables)
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Values stored in MemoryStack from external sources, then resolved recursively
mitigations: Circular reference detection via thread-static visited set
gaps: ResolveDeep() has no skipInfrastructure parameter — always resolves %!var% infrastructure variables

attack_surface.trust_boundary: File paths resolved from user variables — if variables contain external data, paths are attacker-influenced
mitigations: ValidatePath checks path starts with RootDirectory
IPLangFileSystem abstraction prevents direct System.IO usage
Error messages use path.Raw (user-provided), not absolute paths

attack_surface.exposure: Module and action resolution from .pr file JSON
attack_surface.trust_boundary: .pr files are LLM-generated and considered trusted (trust boundary)
mitigations: String-based lookup against curated module registry — no arbitrary type instantiation
Parameter conversion uses whitelist of ~50 safe types
JSON deserialization is strongly typed (no $type polymorphism)
Event bindings resolve to registered goal names only
gaps: module.add/provider.load use Assembly.LoadFrom without path boundary check (accepted risk — .pr is trust boundary)

findings.vector: HTTP file download via StreamWithProgressAsync writes to disk with no size limit.
findings.preconditions: User's PLang app downloads a file from an attacker-controlled or compromised server
findings.impact: Disk exhaustion — attacker serves multi-GB response, fills disk
findings.proposed_fix: Add maxBytes parameter to StreamWithProgressAsync, default to DefaultMaxResponseSize.
findings.fix_verification: maxBytes parameter added (line 957), checked after each chunk (line 976). Configurable via MaxDownloadSize config key (line 188). Correct.

findings.vector: SSE stream handler clears buffer on overflow but keeps connection open — repeated 10MB allocations.
findings.preconditions: User's PLang app connects to SSE endpoint controlled by attacker
findings.impact: Memory pressure via repeated allocation/deallocation cycles.
findings.proposed_fix: Add overflow counter, disconnect after N consecutive overflows.
findings.fix_verification: consecutiveOverflows counter (line 847), disconnects after 3 overflows (line 867). Resets on successful event (line 885). Correct.

findings.vector: HTTP stream reads with no per-read timeout — slow-loris attack sends 1 byte per interval.
findings.preconditions: User's PLang app makes HTTP request to attacker-controlled server
findings.impact: Thread/connection exhaustion via indefinitely held connections.
findings.proposed_fix: Add minimum throughput check: 1KB/sec over 30s sliding window.
findings.fix_verification: Throughput check in ReadLimitedStringAsync (line 307), ReadLimitedBytesAsync (line 340), StreamWithProgressAsync (line 984). All three use 30s window with 1KB/sec threshold. Correct.

findings.vector: FluidProvider UnsafeMemberAccessStrategy + callGoal tag + app in AmbientValues = SSTI if external data enters templates.
findings.preconditions: Template variables include data from external sources.
findings.impact: Information disclosure, goal execution via callGoal.
findings.proposed_fix: Document risk. Consider SafeMemberAccessStrategy for external-data templates.

findings.vector: JsonStringNavigator unwraps JSON without element count limits — millions of tiny items amplify memory.
findings.preconditions: External JSON data stored in a PLang variable, then navigated
findings.fix_verification: MaxElementCount = 100,000 (line 12), ref counter passed through UnwrapElement/UnwrapObject/UnwrapArray. Throws JsonException on exceed. Correct.

findings.vector: JsonStringNavigator.UnwrapElement() recurses without explicit depth guard.
findings.impact: StackOverflowException if depth exceeds CLR stack limit.
findings.fix_verification: MaxDepth = 64 (line 13), depth parameter threaded through all three methods, checked at entry of UnwrapElement (line 50). Correct.

findings.vector: Ed25519Provider header verification uses non-constant-time string.Equals — timing side-channel.
findings.preconditions: Attacker can make repeated verification requests and measure timing
findings.impact: Header value brute-force if headers contain secrets.
findings.proposed_fix: Use CryptographicOperations.FixedTimeEquals() on header value bytes.
findings.fix_verification: UTF-8 encode both values (lines 110-111), compare via CryptographicOperations.FixedTimeEquals (line 112). Correct.

findings.vector: Nonce replay protection uses in-memory cache — process restart allows replay.
findings.preconditions: Attacker captures valid signed request, process restarts.
findings.impact: Replay attack within timeout window after restart.
findings.proposed_fix: Bound replay window via timestamp check.
findings.fix_verification: Cache TTL = effectiveTimeout (line 89). Timestamp check (step 2, line 78) rejects signatures older than effectiveTimeout. After restart, only signatures within the timeout window could replay. Comment documents this (lines 85-86). Correct.

findings.vector: DefaultFileProvider error messages include absolute filesystem paths.
findings.preconditions: Attacker can trigger file operations that fail and observe error messages
findings.impact: Attacker learns absolute paths, OS, username, directory structure.
findings.proposed_fix: Use path.Raw instead of path.Absolute in error messages.
findings.fix_verification: Line 39: 'File not found: {path.Raw}' — no absolute path. Correct.

findings.vector: ResolveDeep has no breadth guard — wide nested structures cause O(N*M) allocations.
findings.preconditions: External data with many keys stored in PLang variable, then resolved
findings.impact: CPU/memory exhaustion via wide object graphs.
findings.proposed_fix: Add breadth counter with MaxResolveItems limit.
findings.fix_verification: MaxResolveItems = 100,000 (line 377), _resolveItemCount incremented per item (line 394), resets at top-level call (line 384). Returns value unchanged when exceeded (soft limit). Correct.

findings.vector: HTTP URL resolution accepts any URL scheme — file://, gopher://, dict:// pass through to HttpClient.
findings.preconditions: User's PLang app makes HTTP request with attacker-influenced URL
findings.impact: Local file read via SSRF on platforms where HttpClient supports file://.
findings.proposed_fix: Validate URL scheme — only allow http:// and https://.
findings.fix_verification: Uri.TryCreate + scheme check (lines 488-493). Blocks all non-http/https schemes. Correct.

findings.vector: ObjectNavigator error messages expose CLR type names via GetType().Name.
findings.preconditions: Attacker can trigger navigation errors and observe error messages
findings.impact: Minor info disclosure — attacker learns internal type structure.
findings.fix_verification: Line 28: 'Failed to read '{key}': {message}' — no type name. Correct.

findings.vector: ResolveDeep() has no skipInfrastructure parameter — always resolves %!var% infrastructure variables even on values from external data. If HTTP response body contains '%!app.System.SettingsStore%' and is stored in a variable, subsequent ResolveDeep call would resolve it.
findings.preconditions: External data (HTTP response, file content) stored in variable contains %!varName% patterns, and that variable is later passed through ResolveDeep
findings.impact: Information disclosure — attacker-controlled strings can read infrastructure state including settings, engine internals.
findings.exploit_sketch:
1. HTTP response contains string: '%!app.System.SettingsStore%'
2. PLang app stores: set %data% = %response.body%
3. Later step resolves %data% — ResolveDeep hits the string
4. Pattern ^%([^%]+)%$ matches, resolves %!app.System.SettingsStore%
5. Infrastructure guard only exists in Resolve(), not ResolveDeep()
findings.proposed_fix: Add skipInfrastructure parameter to ResolveDeep(). Default true when resolving values that originated from external sources.

findings.vector: Slow-loris throughput threshold (1KB/sec) is generous — allows connections held open at very low data rates. At 1KB/sec, a 100MB download takes ~28 hours.
findings.preconditions: Attacker sends data just above 1KB/sec threshold
findings.impact: Connection slot exhaustion with small number of slow connections.
findings.proposed_fix: Consider making the throughput threshold configurable. Document the default.

findings.vector: No SSRF protection against internal IP ranges. http://169.254.169.254 (cloud metadata), http://127.0.0.1, http://10.x.x.x all pass URL scheme check.
findings.preconditions: Attacker controls URL variable, PLang app runs in cloud environment
findings.impact: Cloud metadata theft, internal service discovery.
findings.proposed_fix: Add optional SSRF IP filtering (default off for user-sovereign model, available as config for server-mode apps).

summary: All 11 open findings from v1 have been correctly fixed. 3 new findings from fresh-eyes scan: 1 MEDIUM (ResolveDeep infrastructure variable leak), 2 LOW (throughput threshold, SSRF IP filtering). No critical or high findings remain open. Security posture is strong.
